headline: Automation doesn’t eliminate vague objectives
keyNotes: Even though automated systems (like those that manage ads or customer interactions) will do exactly what you ask, that doesn't always mean they're doing what your business truly needs. For instance, asking for more sign-ups might just fill your system with people who aren't serious about buying.

Important numbers you track, called metrics (like how much money you make from ads, known as ROAS, or how much it costs to get a new customer, called CAC), can look great, but this can be misleading if your initial goal was unclear. The article shows how a metric can improve while the business itself doesn't actually benefit.

To guide automation effectively, you must define clear boundaries, like "sidelines on a playing field," specifying not only what a successful outcome looks like but also what counts as a failure. For example, you might decide it's acceptable for your ad returns (ROAS) to drop from 8x to 5x if it leads to a significant increase in new customers.

The human role isn't to approve every tiny change an automated system makes, but to set the overall rules and boundaries for it, deciding what trade-offs the business is willing to accept and identifying "wins" that might look good on paper but don't truly help the company.

If you automate a process that's built on an untested assumption (like believing a specific customer action will make them stay longer), you're not making the process smarter; you're just making a guess run automatically on a schedule.
question: The article suggests that for any automated system to truly help, you need to define clear boundaries, like a "floor" (a minimum acceptable level) or "sidelines" (what counts as a win and what counts as a loss). Thinking about your own work, where might you be tracking a number that looks good on its own, but isn't necessarily pushing your team or business toward its most important goals, and what specific "floor" or "sideline" could you establish to ensure your efforts are genuinely effective?
